incident.title: Credential Stuffing Attack  -  Token Theft  -  Lateral Movement via WMI
incident.description: Multiple failed logins from two Tor/VPN exit nodes preceded successful authentication for john.doe@contoso.com. An OAuth access token was immediately requested and used to enumerate mailbox contents. Defender telemetry shows subsequent WMI remote execution from the compromised workstation to two additional hosts.
alert_names: Credential Stuffing  -  High Volume Failed Logins

investigation.summary: Confirmed credential stuffing attack from dual Tor/VPN infrastructure targeting john.doe@contoso.com. Successful authentication without MFA was immediately followed by OAuth token acquisition and mailbox enumeration. The compromised workstation then performed WMI-based lateral movement to two unmanaged machines (WORKSTATION-B, WORKSTATION-C), suggesting a precursor to ransomware deployment or data exfiltration staging.
attack_timeline: 10:10:03Z  -  847 failed login attempts begin from Tor exit node 185.220.101.45 (NL) against john.doe@contoso.com
10:11:14Z  -  Secondary credential stuffing from VPN IP 91.108.4.200 (RU) observed in parallel
10:12:33Z  -  Successful authentication for john.doe@contoso.com from 185.220.101.45  -  no MFA challenge issued (user not registered for MFA)
10:13:01Z  -  Attacker immediately requests OAuth delegated permissions grant for Microsoft Graph  -  token issued
10:13:45Z  -  Graph API calls observed: MailFolders.Read, Files.Read.All  -  mailbox and OneDrive enumeration
10:18:44Z  -  WMI remote process creation from DESKTOP-JD-001 to WORKSTATION-B: encoded PowerShell payload executed
10:19:12Z  -  WORKSTATION-B performs WMI remote process creation to WORKSTATION-C: lateral spread confirmed
10:24:00Z  -  Sentinel detects lateral movement pattern; incident created at severity High

mitre_mappings.evidence: 847 failed logins from known Tor exit node followed by success  -  no MFA barrier
OAuth delegated permission grant added immediately after successful login; Graph token used for MailFolders.Read

mitre_mappings.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
mitre_mappings.evidence: OAuth token used to access Microsoft Graph API resources bypassing re-authentication
WMI remote process creation from DESKTOP-JD-001 → WORKSTATION-B → WORKSTATION-C with encoded PowerShell payload
Base64-encoded PowerShell commands launched via WMI on target workstations

investigation.analyst_notes: No MFA on this account was a critical enabler. Lateral movement payload is Base64-encoded PowerShell  -  de-obfuscation recommended to assess stage 2 intent (likely ransomware staging or exfil beacon). Two unmanaged workstations (WORKSTATION-B/C) are also at risk. Session revocation and device isolation are the immediate priorities.

response.summary: Credential stuffing from dual Tor/VPN infrastructure (185.220.101.45, 91.108.4.200) successfully compromised john.doe@contoso.com (no MFA). Attacker obtained OAuth tokens and pivoted laterally via WMI to WORKSTATION-B and WORKSTATION-C using encoded PowerShell.
response.attack_narrative: The attacker operated a dual-source credential stuffing campaign  -  847 failed attempts from the Tor exit node 185.220.101.45 and a parallel stream from Russian VPN 91.108.4.200. The account john.doe@contoso.com was protected only by a password (MFA not registered), allowing the attacker to authenticate without challenge. Within 28 seconds of success, a new delegated OAuth permission was added for Microsoft Graph, granting access to mail and files. The attacker then used WMI to execute Base64-encoded PowerShell on WORKSTATION-B, which in turn spread to WORKSTATION-C  -  classic two-hop lateral movement likely establishing a staging foothold. Both workstations are unmanaged (not Intune-enrolled), increasing attacker dwell-time risk.
response.analyst_notes: Immediate: revoke sessions + disable account. Isolate DESKTOP-JD-001 and WORKSTATION-B. Decode PowerShell payloads for stage-2 intent. Remediation: enforce MFA for all accounts; enroll WORKSTATION-B/C in Intune; review OAuth consent grant policies.

recommended_actions.note: Revoke all active sessions  -  OAuth token remains valid until revoked
Disable account until investigation is complete and MFA is enforced

request_body.Comment: Credential stuffing origin host  -  lateral movement source

recommended_actions.note: Isolate DESKTOP-JD-001  -  confirmed origin of WMI lateral movement

request_body.Comment: Lateral movement target  -  encoded PowerShell executed

recommended_actions.note: Isolate WORKSTATION-B  -  WMI lateral movement target, unmanaged device

echo_rule.earliest_signal: 10:10:03Z  -  First failed login from 185.220.101.45 (Tor exit node)
echo_rule.rule_name: TopSec-ECHO: Early Tor Credential Stuffing Detection
echo_rule.kql_query:
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(5m)
| where ResultType != "0"
| where NetworkLocationDetails has "tor" or ipv4_is_in_range(IPAddress, "185.220.0.0/16")
| summarize FailCount = count(), Accounts = dcount(UserPrincipalName) by IPAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where FailCount > 5
| project TimeGenerated, IPAddress, FailCount, Accounts
| extend Severity = iff(FailCount > 50, "High", "Medium")
